heroOverview: Myalgia — pain arising from skeletal muscle — is one of the most prevalent symptoms in medicine, reported by an estimated 15-33% of adults in population surveys at any given time. Unlike joint pain, myalgia originates in the muscle tissue itself and spans a wide clinical spectrum: from the self-limiting aching of a viral illness or unaccustomed exercise to the persistent, widespread pain of inflammatory myopathies, medication side effects, or systemic disease. The character and distribution of the pain, its relationship to exertion and rest, and associated features such as weakness, rash, fever, or dark urine are the most diagnostically informative clues. Most acute myalgia resolves within days to weeks without specific treatment; identifying the minority of cases caused by progressive myositis, rhabdomyolysis, or an underlying inflammatory or endocrine disorder prevents serious complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Reported by 15-33% of adults in population surveys; among the top five reasons for general practice consultations internationally (WHO primary care data)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; inflammatory myopathies peak between ages 40-60; statin myalgia affects ~5-10% of statin users; exercise-induced myalgia is universal
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; cause determines the demographic: viral myalgia more common in younger adults, polymyalgia rheumatica almost exclusively affects those over 50
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence; back pain alone — a major category of myalgia — affects approximately 540 million people at any given time (Lancet, 2018)

definition: Myalgia (ICD-10: M79.1) is pain or tenderness localised to one or more skeletal muscles, classified by duration (acute versus chronic) and by distribution (focal versus diffuse/widespread). The International Association for the Study of Pain defines myalgia broadly as pain originating from muscle tissue, which must be distinguished clinically from arthralgia (joint pain), neuropathic pain, and referred visceral pain. Focal myalgia typically points to a local cause: muscle overuse, direct trauma, infection, or myofascial trigger point. Diffuse myalgia affecting multiple muscle groups simultaneously raises a wider differential including viral illness, fibromyalgia, inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis, dermatomyositis), metabolic muscle disease, medication side effects (most notably statin therapy), endocrine disorders (hypothyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, Addison's disease), electrolyte abnormalities (hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia), and systemic connective tissue disease. Myalgia itself is a symptom, not a diagnosis; its underlying cause must be identified for targeted management. The term 'myalgia' falls under rheumatology, sports medicine, and orthopaedics depending on context, and may be managed by a GP for most straightforward cases.

typesClassification.description: Universal, self-limiting muscle aching peaking 24-72 hours after unaccustomed eccentric exercise; caused by micro-tears in muscle fibres and subsequent inflammatory repair; resolves within 5-7 days without treatment.

Diffuse deep muscle aching accompanying influenza, COVID-19, enteroviruses, and other systemic infections; driven by circulating inflammatory cytokines and direct viral invasion; resolves with the underlying illness.

Progressive proximal muscle weakness with myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and inflammatory infiltrate on biopsy; includes polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and inclusion body myositis; requires immunosuppressive treatment.

Muscle aching caused by medications, most commonly statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors), affecting approximately 5-10% of users; ranges from mild myalgia to severe rhabdomyolysis depending on dose and drug interactions.

Myalgia secondary to hypothyroidism, hypoadrenalism, Cushing's syndrome, or electrolyte disturbances; characteristically improves substantially with correction of the underlying metabolic abnormality.

Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain at characteristic tender points without objective muscle pathology on biopsy or blood tests; associated with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive difficulties.

primarySymptoms: Diffuse or focal muscle aching, often described as a deep, dull, or heavy sensation rather than sharp pain, which may be constant or worsen with use of the affected muscle.
Muscle tenderness to direct palpation — a consistent physical finding that distinguishes myalgia from most neuropathic or referred pain syndromes.
Muscle stiffness on waking or after prolonged inactivity, lasting minutes to hours depending on the underlying cause; prolonged morning stiffness (over 60 minutes) suggests an inflammatory process.
Reduced range of motion or voluntary muscle contraction because of pain, particularly noticeable when climbing stairs, rising from a chair, or lifting the arms above the head in proximal myopathy.
Muscle swelling or firmness of the affected group in acute injury, overuse, or rhabdomyolysis.
Weakness out of proportion to pain — a key finding in inflammatory myopathies, where objective power reduction exists regardless of whether the muscles hurt at rest.
Fatigue that is disproportionate to activity level, particularly in systemic causes such as hypothyroidism, connective tissue disease, or post-viral syndromes.
Dark, tea-coloured urine (myoglobinuria) in rhabdomyolysis, indicating massive muscle cell breakdown with myoglobin spilling into the urine — an emergency sign.
Fever, malaise, and lymphadenopathy accompanying myalgia in viral, bacterial, or autoimmune inflammatory states.
Skin rash — heliotrope rash around the eyes or Gottron's papules on the knuckles — in dermatomyositis, which may precede or accompany profound proximal muscle weakness.
Joint aches coexisting with myalgia in rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, and other connective tissue diseases, where distinguishing the sources of pain requires systematic examination.
earlyWarningSigns: New muscle aching within days of starting or increasing a statin, fibrate, or other potentially myotoxic medication.
Persistent muscle pain beyond 7-10 days after an acute viral illness, which may signal a post-viral myositis.
Progressive difficulty climbing stairs, rising from sitting, or raising arms above shoulder level over weeks, suggesting evolving proximal myopathy.
Unexplained fatigue and cold intolerance accompanying myalgia, pointing toward hypothyroidism, which is one of the most treatable and commonly missed causes.
emergencySigns: Dark brown or red-tinged urine with severe diffuse muscle pain and swelling — rhabdomyolysis with acute myoglobinuria that can cause kidney failure within hours.
Acute severe muscle weakness affecting breathing or swallowing, which may indicate fulminant myositis or hypokalaemic paralysis.
High fever, rigors, and muscle rigidity after recent antipsychotic exposure — possible neuroleptic malignant syndrome, a medical emergency.
Severe myalgia with hypotension and arrhythmia after excessive exertion or extreme temperature exposure — exertional heat stroke with rhabdomyolysis.
Sudden onset severe proximal weakness with inability to stand or walk — acute inflammatory myopathy requiring urgent investigation.

causes.description: Influenza A and B, COVID-19, enteroviruses, and HIV directly invade muscle cells and trigger systemic cytokine release (interleukin-6, tumour necrosis factor-alpha) that mediates diffuse myalgia; almost all adult febrile illnesses produce some degree of muscle aching through this inflammatory mechanism.

Unaccustomed or intense eccentric muscle contractions — as in downhill running or heavy resistance training — create micro-tears in sarcomeres and Z-lines that trigger a local inflammatory cascade, producing delayed-onset muscle soreness peaking at 24-72 hours and resolving within 5-7 days as repair is completed.

Statin medications inhibit CoQ10 synthesis and impair mitochondrial function in skeletal muscle, causing myalgia in roughly 5-10% of users; risk rises substantially with high-dose statins, co-prescription of CYP3A4 inhibitors (diltiazem, azithromycin, grapefruit), and genetic variants in SLCO1B1 and RYR1 genes that increase statin accumulation in muscle.

Autoimmune conditions — polymyositis, dermatomyositis, necrotising autoimmune myopathy, and inclusion body myositis — produce T-cell and complement-mediated muscle fibre destruction, causing progressive proximal weakness with elevated creatine kinase, and myalgia that persists at rest as well as on exertion.

Thyroid hormone deficiency impairs muscle energy metabolism, slows sarcolemmal sodium-potassium ATPase activity, and reduces mitochondrial oxidative capacity, producing myalgia, cramps, proximal weakness, and elevated creatine kinase in approximately 30-40% of hypothyroid patients; all features reverse with thyroid hormone replacement.

Hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, and hypomagnesaemia alter resting membrane potential and neuromuscular excitability; even moderate degrees produce muscle cramps, aching, and weakness that resolve once the deficiency is corrected.

Massive breakdown of skeletal muscle cells from extreme exertion, crush injury, hyperthermia, ischaemia, or toxic agents releases myoglobin and cellular contents into the bloodstream, causing severe diffuse myalgia, profound weakness, swelling, and dark urine; acute kidney injury follows in 10-50% of serious cases.

causes.cause: Connective tissue disease and autoimmune disorders
causes.description: Systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren's syndrome, and mixed connective tissue disease produce immune complex deposition, vasculitis, and inflammatory infiltrates in muscle, causing myalgia that is typically accompanied by joint symptoms, constitutional features, and specific autoantibodies on blood testing.

riskFactors.factor: Statin use at high dose or with interacting drugs
riskFactors.description: Simvastatin 80 mg and rosuvastatin at high doses carry the highest myotoxic risk; co-prescribing CYP3A4 inhibitors or fibrates multiplies risk; switching to a lower dose or pravastatin (not CYP3A4-metabolised) reduces it.

Rapid increases in training load, particularly eccentric-dominant activities in untrained individuals, reliably produce DOMS; graduated conditioning over weeks substantially reduces the severity of subsequent episodes.

Pre-existing lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, or other autoimmune conditions increase the risk of developing an inflammatory myopathy; approximately 20-30% of myositis patients have overlapping connective tissue disease.

Undiagnosed or inadequately treated hypothyroidism is among the most reversible causes of myalgia; thyroid-stimulating hormone screening identifies the cause in a subset of patients with unexplained muscle aching and weakness.

Fibromyalgia affects women approximately 7 times more than men; dermatomyositis and lupus-related myalgia are also more prevalent in women. Polymyalgia rheumatica has a 2:1 female predominance and almost exclusively affects those over 50.

riskFactors.factor: Genetic variants in SLCO1B1 and RYR1
riskFactors.description: SLCO1B1 gene variants reduce hepatic statin uptake, raising plasma statin concentration in muscle; RYR1 variants predispose to malignant hyperthermia and exertional rhabdomyolysis; genetic testing is available for unexplained severe statin-related or exertional myopathy.

Chronic heavy alcohol consumption produces alcoholic myopathy in roughly 30-50% of alcohol-dependent individuals via mitochondrial toxicity and protein synthesis impairment; acute alcoholic myopathy with rhabdomyolysis can follow binge episodes.

Serum vitamin D below 25 nmol/L is consistently associated with musculoskeletal pain, proximal weakness, and fatigue; supplementation resolves myalgia attributable to deficiency in the majority of affected patients.

affectedDemographics: Adults of all ages following viral illness (the most common cause worldwide)
Athletes and individuals starting new exercise programmes
Adults aged 40-80 on statin therapy (5-10% experience clinically meaningful myalgia)
Women aged 40-60 with autoimmune inflammatory myopathy
Adults over 50 with polymyalgia rheumatica (almost exclusively affects this age group)
Individuals with undiagnosed hypothyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, or electrolyte disorders
People with chronic alcohol use disorder
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing myalgia begins with a detailed history focused on onset, duration, distribution, severity, relationship to exercise and rest, associated symptoms, and a complete drug and supplement list. Acute diffuse myalgia following a flu-like illness in a young person rarely needs investigation beyond clinical assessment. Red flags that prompt systematic testing include: myalgia persisting beyond 2-3 weeks without an obvious cause; progressive proximal weakness; very dark urine; a new or recently up-titrated statin; morning stiffness lasting more than an hour; skin rash; or constitutional symptoms such as unintentional weight loss. Serum creatine kinase (CK) is the primary blood test, elevated in rhabdomyolysis (often more than 10 times the upper limit of normal), inflammatory myopathy (5-50 times normal), and moderate statin myopathy (3-10 times). Normal CK with myalgia points toward fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, medication effect at low dose, or functional pain rather than a destructive myopathy. Additional first-line tests include thyroid-stimulating hormone, full blood count, electrolytes, renal function, and ESR/CRP. In suspected inflammatory myopathy, myositis-specific autoantibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-MDA5, anti-Mi-2), aldolase, and inflammatory markers supplement CK. Muscle MRI identifies the distribution and severity of active inflammation, guiding biopsy site selection. Electromyography distinguishes myopathic from neuropathic patterns. Muscle biopsy remains the diagnostic gold standard for inflammatory myopathies when non-invasive tests are equivocal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary marker of muscle cell breakdown; elevated levels indicate myopathy, inflammatory muscle disease, or rhabdomyolysis; normal CK myalgia points toward fibromyalgia or metabolic causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results are available within a few hours; strenuous exercise within 48 hours should be avoided before the test as it falsely elevates CK.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hypothyroidism, one of the most common reversible causes of myalgia, proximal weakness, and elevated CK; a single test will detect overt hypothyroidism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within a few hours. If TSH is elevated, free T4 is added to quantify the degree of deficiency.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, and hypomagnesaemia as reversible metabolic causes of myalgia and cramps; renal function is essential when rhabdomyolysis is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood panel from a single venous sample; results within 2-4 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: ESR, CRP, and full blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses systemic inflammation; markedly elevated ESR (often >100 mm/h) with normal CK in an adult over 50 strongly suggests polymyalgia rheumatica.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies inflammatory myopathy subtypes and predicts clinical course; anti-Jo-1 antibodies indicate antisynthetase syndrome with interstitial lung disease risk; anti-MDA5 indicates risk of rapidly progressive lung disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw; results take 5-14 days depending on the laboratory; specialist interpretation is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the distribution of muscle oedema and fatty infiltration in inflammatory myopathy, guides biopsy site selection, and monitors treatment response without radiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in an MRI scanner for 30-60 minutes; gadolinium contrast may be given for active inflammatory assessment; no radiation involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive histological diagnosis of inflammatory myopathy, metabolic myopathy, or mitochondrial disease when non-invasive tests are insufficient or when treatment decision depends on subtype.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small incision under local anaesthesia, usually in the vastus lateralis or deltoid; a tissue core is removed; the procedure takes 20-30 minutes and leaves a small scar.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for myalgia is directed at the underlying cause rather than at the symptom itself. Most acute myalgia — whether from exercise, viral illness, or a brief medication side effect — resolves within 5-10 days with rest, adequate hydration, and simple analgesics. When a modifiable cause is identified — hypothyroidism, statin use, electrolyte deficiency, or vitamin D deficiency — correcting it resolves the myalgia in the majority of patients within 4-12 weeks. Statin-induced myalgia should prompt CK measurement, temporary statin hold, and either dose reduction or switching to a lower-potency or pharmacokinetically distinct statin once symptoms resolve. Inflammatory myopathies require immunosuppressive treatment: high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily is the standard starting regimen, producing objective CK normalisation and strength improvement in 70-80% of polymyositis cases, with azathioprine or methotrexate added as steroid-sparing agents for long-term control. Polymyalgia rheumatica responds dramatically to moderate doses of prednisolone — typically 15 mg daily — with symptom resolution within 24-72 hours confirming the diagnosis. Rhabdomyolysis demands hospital admission for aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation at 200-1,000 mL/hour to protect the kidneys and clear myoglobin, targeting urine output of at least 200-300 mL/hour. Fibromyalgia is managed with a combination of graded aerobic exercise (the most evidence-based intervention, producing clinically meaningful pain reduction in approximately 30-40% of patients), cognitive behavioural therapy, and selected pharmacotherapy such as duloxetine, milnacipran, or pregabalin.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen, naproxen)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesics for acute myalgia from exercise, viral illness, or minor trauma; inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and reduce local inflammatory muscle pain within hours; used for 3-10 days at standard doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces acute myalgia intensity by approximately 25-35% versus placebo in randomised trials; particularly effective for DOMS and post-viral myalgia.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (glucocorticoids)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line immunosuppression for inflammatory myopathies and polymyalgia rheumatica; high-dose (1 mg/kg) for polymyositis and dermatomyositis, moderate dose (15 mg) for PMR; tapered over months to years guided by CK and clinical response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CK normalisation and functional improvement in approximately 70-80% of patients with polymyositis or dermatomyositis; near-universal dramatic response within 72 hours in polymyalgia rheumatica.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine or methotrexate (steroid-sparing agents)
medicalTreatments.description: Added 3-6 months after steroid initiation to allow tapering and minimise long-term steroid side effects in inflammatory myopathy; both require monitoring of blood count, liver enzymes, and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allows successful prednisolone reduction in approximately 60-70% of inflammatory myopathy patients when used as adjunct therapy in published randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Thyroid hormone replacement (levothyroxine)
medicalTreatments.description: For hypothyroid myalgia: restoring TSH to the normal range resolves myalgia, cramps, proximal weakness, and elevated CK, often within 4-12 weeks of adequate replacement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Myalgia and CK normalisation in approximately 80-90% of patients with hypothyroid myopathy who achieve euthyroid status on adequate levothyroxine.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine or pregabalin for fibromyalgia
medicalTreatments.description: Duloxetine (a serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor) and pregabalin (a calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligand) are FDA-approved for fibromyalgia; they modulate central pain sensitisation and produce clinically meaningful pain reduction in a subset of patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show approximately 30% of fibromyalgia patients achieve a clinically meaningful pain reduction (50% or more) with duloxetine; comparable results with pregabalin.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous fluid resuscitation for rhabdomyolysis
medicalTreatments.description: Aggressive IV normal saline or Ringer's lactate at 200-1,000 mL/hour in hospital to clear myoglobin from the tubules and prevent acute kidney injury; treatment continues until CK is falling and urine is clear.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early aggressive hydration reduces acute kidney injury risk from approximately 30-50% to below 10% in most rhabdomyolysis series when initiated before kidney damage is established.

alternativeTreatments.name: Graded aerobic exercise programme
alternativeTreatments.description: The most robustly evidence-based treatment for fibromyalgia: low-impact aerobic activity (swimming, walking, cycling) starting at low intensity and progressing gradually reduces pain, improves function, and reduces fatigue more sustainably than pharmacotherapy alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Massage therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Soft-tissue massage reduces acute myalgia intensity and improves range of motion in sport-related and overuse myalgia; a 2018 Cochrane review found moderate evidence of short-term benefit for acute low back myalgia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Heat and cold therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Local heat relaxes muscle spasm and increases local blood flow in subacute and chronic myalgia; cold packs in the first 24-48 hours of acute muscle injury reduce inflammatory oedema and pain.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: For vitamin D-deficient patients with myalgia: restoring serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D above 50 nmol/L resolves musculoskeletal pain in a significant proportion; a dose of 1,000-2,000 IU daily or equivalent weekly loading is typically used.

whySeeSpecialist: Most self-limiting myalgia — from viral illness, exercise, or minor trauma — can be managed in primary care. Referral to a rheumatologist or orthopaedic physician is indicated when myalgia persists beyond 4-6 weeks without a clear cause, when progressive proximal weakness is present, when inflammatory markers are significantly elevated, when myositis-specific antibodies are positive, or when first-line empirical treatment has not led to improvement. Rhabdomyolysis and suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome require emergency hospital admission and are not suitable for outpatient management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a rheumatologist for suspected inflammatory myopathy or polymyalgia rheumatica, who has access to muscle MRI, autoantibody panels, and electrodiagnostic testing. For statin myalgia or metabolic causes, a GP with a good understanding of myotoxic drug interactions can manage most cases. Fibromyalgia benefits from a multidisciplinary team including a physiotherapist experienced in graded exercise, a pain specialist, and a psychologist trained in pain-focused CBT. Ask specifically whether the rheumatologist has experience with complex myositis or with monitoring immunosuppressive therapy.
preventionStrategies: Increase exercise intensity gradually — the 10% rule (increase load by no more than 10% per week) prevents exercise-induced muscle injury.
Request a CK check before and shortly after starting or up-titrating a statin, and report any muscle symptoms promptly to prescribing clinicians.
Screen for and correct vitamin D deficiency, hypothyroidism, and electrolyte abnormalities at presentation with unexplained myalgia.
Avoid abrupt large increases in training volume in untrained or recently deconditioned individuals.
Ensure adequate hydration and cooling in hot environments during sustained exertion to reduce exertional rhabdomyolysis risk.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a regular graduated exercise programme — muscles that are regularly conditioned are more resistant to strain-related and inflammatory myalgia.
Stay well hydrated, particularly around strenuous activity, since dehydration accelerates muscle cell damage during exertion.
Ensure adequate sleep, as muscle repair and growth hormone secretion occur predominantly during deep sleep.
Reduce alcohol intake, especially for patients with drug-induced or metabolic myalgia, since alcohol amplifies myotoxicity.
Use ergonomic workstation setups to prevent postural overuse myalgia of the neck, shoulders, and lower back.
Keep a symptom diary relating pain episodes to activity, medications, and dietary intake to identify modifiable triggers.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.6 g/kg body weight daily) to support muscle repair and maintenance
Vitamin D-rich foods (oily fish, eggs, fortified dairy) and supplementation if serum levels are below 50 nmol/L
Generous fluid intake before, during, and after exercise to support muscle perfusion and reduce breakdown products
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, leafy greens, legumes) to prevent deficiency-related muscle cramps and aching
Anti-inflammatory dietary pattern (Mediterranean-style with olive oil, oily fish, vegetables) as a supportive measure in inflammatory myopathy
avoid: Grapefruit and grapefruit juice in patients on statins, since it inhibits CYP3A4 and significantly raises statin plasma levels and myotoxicity risk
Excess alcohol, which acts synergistically with myotoxic medications and directly impairs muscle protein synthesis
Very low-calorie diets that risk electrolyte deficiencies driving muscle cramps and aching

exerciseGuidelines: For exercise-induced myalgia, relative rest and gentle movement (not complete immobilisation) speed recovery. Return to full training gradually over 3-7 days once soreness has resolved. In inflammatory myopathy, exercise was previously discouraged but current guidelines (EULAR/ACR) recommend graded supervised exercise even during active disease, as it does not worsen inflammation and preserves function. In fibromyalgia, consistent low-impact aerobic exercise 3-5 times weekly is the single most effective long-term intervention. Patients with rhabdomyolysis should avoid vigorous exercise until CK has returned to near-normal, typically 2-4 weeks after an acute episode.
dailyManagement: For statin users: report any new muscle pain or weakness promptly and do not self-stop statins without medical advice — a hold with CK measurement is preferred to unilateral discontinuation.
Take prescribed immunosuppressant doses consistently and attend blood-monitoring appointments — missed doses or monitoring lead to disease flare or undetected toxicity.
Grade physical activity on a pain scale (0-10) daily and share these records with the treating clinician to track trends.
Apply heat before activity and ice after vigorous exercise to manage post-exertional myalgia.
Recognise dark urine as an emergency that requires same-day medical attention — myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis can progress to kidney failure rapidly.
prognosis: Prognosis is highly cause-dependent. Exercise-induced and viral myalgia are self-limiting, resolving within days to two weeks in healthy individuals with no lasting sequelae. Hypothyroid, vitamin D-deficient, and electrolyte-related myalgia resolves fully once the underlying deficiency is corrected. Statin myalgia resolves within 2-6 weeks of drug discontinuation in most patients and does not cause permanent muscle damage when caught early. Polymyalgia rheumatica responds dramatically to prednisolone and most patients achieve remission within 1-2 years, though relapses occur in 30-50% during steroid taper. Polymyositis and dermatomyositis are more serious: approximately 70-80% of patients achieve functional remission with immunosuppression, but some 20-30% have a relapsing-remitting course or develop significant complications including interstitial lung disease. Rhabdomyolysis carries a low mortality when treated early but acute kidney injury occurs in 10-50% of severe cases; most patients recover full renal function. Fibromyalgia is chronic and non-progressive but not structurally damaging; long-term multimodal management achieves clinically meaningful improvement in roughly half of patients.
recoveryTimeline: DOMS from exercise resolves in 5-7 days without treatment. Viral myalgia settles with the febrile illness, usually within 7-14 days. Statin myalgia resolves 2-6 weeks after discontinuation. Hypothyroid myalgia improves measurably within 4-8 weeks of adequate levothyroxine and fully resolves by 12 weeks in most patients. Polymyalgia rheumatica pain responds within 24-72 hours of starting prednisolone; steroid tapering spans 1-2 years. Inflammatory myopathy improvement in strength takes 3-6 months of treatment; full functional recovery, where achievable, may take 12-24 months. Rhabdomyolysis muscle pain and swelling resolve over 1-4 weeks as CK normalises.
complications: Rhabdomyolysis progressing to acute kidney injury — the most urgent complication, occurring in 10-50% of severe cases; presents with dark urine, oliguria, and rising creatinine.
Chronic inflammatory myopathy complications: interstitial lung disease (in 20-30% of antisynthetase syndrome), dysphagia from pharyngeal myositis, and cardiac arrhythmia from myocarditis.
Long-term glucocorticoid toxicity from treatment of inflammatory myopathy: osteoporosis, cataracts, hyperglycaemia, hypertension, and susceptibility to infection.
Deconditioning and muscle atrophy from prolonged rest or avoidance of activity in any chronic myalgia syndrome, worsening pain and functional decline.
Functional disability and depression from fibromyalgia or other chronic myalgia, with significant impact on employment and quality of life in a substantial proportion of patients.

supportResources.name: Myositis UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information, support groups, and resources for people with inflammatory myopathies including polymyositis and dermatomyositis.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Myositis information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing disease information from the American College of Rheumatology on inflammatory muscle disease.

supportResources.name: NHS — Fibromyalgia
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guide to fibromyalgia causes, diagnosis, and management options including medication and exercise.

sources.title: Dalakas MC. Inflammatory muscle diseases. New England Journal of Medicine, 2015
Briani C et al. Management of drug-induced myopathy. Drugs, 2016

Aggarwal R et al. 2017 European League Against Rheumatism/American College of Rheumatology classification criteria for adult and juvenile idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2017
Clauw DJ. Fibromyalgia: a clinical review. JAMA, 2014
Bohan A, Peter JB. Polymyositis and dermatomyositis (first of two parts). New England Journal of Medicine, 1975
NICE NG193 — Myositis: assessment and management (2021)
Vanholder R et al. Rhabdomyolysis. Journal of the American Society of Nephrology, 2000

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia is a specific chronic widespread pain syndrome with normal CK and no inflammatory changes on biopsy; it is a diagnosis of exclusion. General myalgia may have an identifiable inflammatory, metabolic, or medication cause that fibromyalgia lacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes marked shoulder and hip-girdle stiffness with very high ESR in adults over 50, with normal CK and no true muscle weakness; it is specifically steroid-responsive. Inflammatory myopathy causes objective weakness with elevated CK.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rhabdomyolysis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rhabdomyolysis is a medical emergency with extreme CK elevation (often >10,000 U/L), dark urine, and risk of kidney failure — it is a severe consequence of muscle breakdown rather than simple myalgia.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Fibromyalgia is a distinct chronic widespread pain syndrome that overlaps clinically with myalgia; many patients are investigated for myalgia before fibromyalgia is diagnosed as the primary condition.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Polymyalgia rheumatica is the most important specific inflammatory cause of proximal myalgia in adults over 50, requiring distinct steroid treatment and temporal artery assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: Dermatomyositis is the most clinically distinctive inflammatory myopathy, combining skin and muscle disease; it is one of the main conditions where myalgia with rash must be evaluated urgently.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: Polymyositis is the inflammatory myopathy presenting with progressive proximal weakness and myalgia without skin involvement, requiring biopsy confirmation and immunosuppressive treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Rhabdomyolysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Rhabdomyolysis is the most dangerous acute complication of severe myalgia, representing massive muscle cell breakdown; recognising it early prevents acute kidney injury.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-exertional myalgia is a hallmark of CFS/ME; the two conditions share overlapping symptom profiles and are commonly evaluated together in specialist settings.

faqs.question: What is myalgia?
faqs.answer: Myalgia means pain or tenderness arising from skeletal muscle. It ranges from self-limiting soreness after exercise to persistent pain from inflammatory muscle disease, medication side effects, or hormonal conditions.

faqs.question: What causes myalgia?
faqs.answer: Common causes include viral illness, unaccustomed exercise, statin medications, hypothyroidism, electrolyte deficiencies, vitamin D deficiency, and inflammatory muscle diseases such as polymyositis. Most acute cases resolve within days.

faqs.question: How long does myalgia last?
faqs.answer: Exercise-induced soreness resolves in 5-7 days. Viral myalgia settles within 7-14 days. Drug-induced cases clear 2-6 weeks after stopping the medication. Inflammatory myopathy requires months of treatment. Fibromyalgia is chronic.

faqs.question: Is myalgia the same as fibromyalgia?
faqs.answer: No. Myalgia is a symptom — muscle pain — that can have many causes. Fibromyalgia is a specific chronic syndrome of widespread pain with tender points, normal blood tests, and no structural muscle abnormality. Fibromyalgia is diagnosed only after other causes of myalgia are excluded.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for myalgia?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if muscle pain persists beyond 2-3 weeks without an obvious cause, if you develop progressive weakness, if your urine turns dark, if you have a skin rash alongside the pain, or if you recently started a statin or other new medication.

faqs.question: Can statins cause myalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statins cause muscle aching in roughly 5-10% of users. Symptoms typically start within weeks of starting therapy or increasing the dose. Stopping the statin resolves the myalgia within 2-6 weeks in most people. Report symptoms to your doctor before stopping.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for myalgia?
faqs.answer: The most important first test is serum creatine kinase (CK), which measures muscle breakdown. Other key tests are thyroid-stimulating hormone, electrolytes, vitamin D, ESR, CRP, and full blood count. Elevated CK points toward muscle damage; normal CK suggests fibromyalgia or a metabolic cause.

faqs.question: What is dark urine a sign of with muscle pain?
faqs.answer: Dark brown or tea-coloured urine with severe muscle pain signals rhabdomyolysis — massive muscle cell breakdown releasing myoglobin into the urine. This is an emergency requiring immediate hospital treatment with intravenous fluids to prevent kidney failure.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for myalgia?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Simple NSAIDs work for exercise or viral myalgia. Stopping a culprit drug resolves drug-induced cases. Thyroid hormone replacement fixes hypothyroid myalgia. Inflammatory myopathy needs prednisolone. Fibromyalgia responds best to graded exercise combined with psychological therapy.

faqs.question: Does exercise help or worsen myalgia?
faqs.answer: For most causes, gentle graduated exercise aids recovery and should not be avoided. For inflammatory myopathy, exercise is encouraged even during active disease. For fibromyalgia, regular low-impact aerobic exercise is the single most effective treatment. Avoid vigorous exercise during acute rhabdomyolysis or severe untreated myositis.

faqs.question: Can hypothyroidism cause muscle pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism causes myalgia, cramps, proximal weakness, and elevated creatine kinase in approximately 30-40% of affected patients. All muscle symptoms typically resolve within 8-12 weeks of starting adequate levothyroxine replacement. TSH screening is part of routine myalgia assessment.

faqs.question: What is polymyalgia rheumatica?
faqs.answer: Polymyalgia rheumatica is a specific inflammatory condition causing severe shoulder and hip girdle stiffness and pain, almost exclusively in adults over 50. ESR is markedly elevated but CK is normal. It responds dramatically — within 24-72 hours — to a moderate dose of prednisolone.

faqs.question: Can viral infections cause muscle pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Influenza, COVID-19, and many other viral infections cause diffuse deep muscle aching through cytokine release and sometimes direct muscle invasion. This typically resolves with the fever within 7-14 days and requires only rest, fluids, and analgesia.

faqs.question: Is vitamin D deficiency linked to myalgia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Low vitamin D is consistently associated with musculoskeletal pain and proximal weakness. Supplementation resolves myalgia in the majority of people whose vitamin D is below 50 nmol/L, making it one of the most straightforward and reversible causes to check.

faqs.question: Can myalgia affect breathing?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Severe inflammatory myopathy affecting respiratory muscles can impair breathing and is a medical emergency. Antisynthetase syndrome can also cause interstitial lung disease alongside myalgia. Shortness of breath with myalgia always needs urgent assessment.

faqs.question: How is inflammatory myopathy treated?
faqs.answer: The standard first-line treatment is high-dose prednisolone, usually 1 mg per kg body weight daily, tapered over months as CK normalises and strength improves. Azathioprine or methotrexate are added as steroid-sparing agents. Severe cases may need intravenous immunoglobulin.

faqs.question: Can myalgia be a symptom of COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Yes. Myalgia affects 30-50% of COVID-19 patients during the acute illness and is among its most common systemic symptoms. Post-COVID syndrome includes persistent muscle pain and fatigue lasting weeks to months after the acute infection resolves in some patients.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myalgia and arthralgia?
faqs.answer: Myalgia is pain arising from muscle tissue, while arthralgia is pain originating in joints. In practice they overlap, and many inflammatory conditions cause both. Distinguishing them requires examining whether the pain is worst with muscle contraction or with passive joint movement.

faqs.question: Does massage help myalgia?
faqs.answer: Moderate evidence supports massage for short-term relief of acute and overuse myalgia, particularly in the back and neck. A 2018 Cochrane review found benefit for acute low back myalgia. It is best used alongside other treatments rather than as a sole intervention.

faqs.question: Can myalgia be permanent?
faqs.answer: Most myalgia is not permanent. Self-limiting causes (viral, exercise, drug-induced) resolve fully. Inflammatory myopathies can leave some residual weakness if scarring occurs before diagnosis and treatment. Fibromyalgia is chronic but does not cause structural muscle damage or permanent weakness.

metaDescription: Myalgia is muscle pain with causes ranging from viral illness and statins to inflammatory myopathy. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.
